children.name: Phoenicopterus
children.description: The greater flamingos — large, pale pink flamingos with the widest distribution of any flamingo genus. They inhabit shallow saline and alkaline lakes across Africa, southern Europe, the Caribbean, and South America. Their pink colour comes from carotenoid pigments in the brine shrimp and algae they filter from the water.

children.name: Phoenicopterus roseus
children.description: The largest and most widespread flamingo, standing up to 150 cm tall. Pale pink plumage with a white eye and a pink bill with a black tip. Found from southern Europe and Africa through the Middle East to India. The Camargue in southern France hosts one of the most important breeding colonies in Europe.

children.name: Phoenicopterus ruber
children.description: The brightest pink flamingo, found in the Caribbean, Yucatán, the Galápagos, and northern South America. Its vivid colour comes from a diet rich in carotenoid pigments. The only flamingo species that regularly occurs in North America, breeding in Cuba, the Bahamas, and the Yucatán.

children.name: Phoenicopterus chilensis
children.description: A medium-sized flamingo of temperate South America, found from Peru and Chile east to Argentina and southern Brazil. Pale pink with greyish legs and distinctive red knee joints. Inhabits shallow brackish lakes and estuaries. Classified as Near Threatened due to egg collection and habitat disturbance.

children.name: Phoeniconaias
children.description: The lesser flamingo — a monotypic genus containing the smallest flamingo species. Despite its small size, it is the most numerous flamingo, with an estimated 2-3 million individuals concentrated in the Great Rift Valley lakes of East Africa.

children.name: Phoeniconaias minor
children.description: The smallest flamingo, reaching 90 cm. Deepest pink of all flamingos, with a dark red bill. Breeds in huge colonies on alkaline lakes in East Africa, particularly Lake Natron in Tanzania. Feeds on Spirulina algae, which gives it its intense colour. Classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Phoenicoparrus
children.description: The Andean flamingos — two species restricted to the high-altitude salt flats and lakes of the Andes Mountains in South America. They are the rarest flamingos, both classified as Vulnerable. They endure extreme conditions including intense UV radiation and nightly freezing temperatures at elevations over 4,000 metres.

children.name: Phoenicoparrus andinus
children.description: The only flamingo with yellow legs and a yellow bill base. Found at high-altitude lakes in the Andes of Peru, Chile, Bolivia, and Argentina. The rarest of the South American flamingos, with fewer than 35,000 individuals. Classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Phoenicoparrus jamesi
children.description: Also known as the Puna Flamingo, found at the highest elevations of any flamingo, up to 4,500 metres. Bright pink body with deep carmine markings on the neck and back, and a distinctive bright yellow bill with a black tip. Named after the British naturalist Harry Berkeley James.
